content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
Ron W. Beals 
213 Delta Oaks Way 
Sacramento, CA 95831 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-040 
Dear Mr. Beals: 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
April 6, 2015 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-governmental 
employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 We are not a finder of fact 
when rendering advice (/11 re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide 
assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act's "permanent ban" prohibit you from working on certain projects for a 
private consultant who will be contracting with your former agency? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The projects on which you will be engaged are different proceedings from the 
projects you worked on during your state employment. 
FACTS 
You retired as Chief Counsel for Caltrans in December 2014, after approximately 6 years 
in the position. You are now considering a position as a consultant for a private company that 
does business with Caltrans. While Chief Counsel for Caltrans, you worked on projects that were 
intended to garner additional revenue for the state. Examples include: 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-15-040 
Page No. 2 
1. A proposal to "privatize" Caltrans Changeable Message Signs (the state-owned signs 
that have messages on current traffic conditions, general safety messages and "Amber Alert" 
type warnings). No comprehensive plan or contract was ever developed. California joined with 
several other states, however, and filed a "SEP-15" request with the Federal Highway 
Administration requesting permission for a trial program. The purpose of the program would be 
for private companies to provide and maintain these signs. continuing to post public service 
messages, but also using the signs for private advertising messages. The Federal Highway 
Administration requested that the states withdraw the proposal, which they did. Nothing further 
has been done regarding this proposal for approximately the past four years 
2. Caltrans hired a private consultant to review Caltrans' properties throughout the state 
and to prepare a report about the commercial feasibility of using such properties for outdoor 
advertising purposes. Properties include Maintenance Offices, Park and Ride Lots, 
Administrative buildings, etc. You attended several meetings with different private companies 
and consultants. Due to lack of interest from the Brown Administration, Caltrans never 
authorized completion/finalization of the report. Again, nothing further has been done regarding 
this proposal approximately for four years. 
You are now considering a consulting position with a private outdoor advertising 
company that intends to participate should the current Administration decide to employ private 
uses of Caltrans right of way for outdoor advertising purposes. 
ANALYSIS 
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. In addition, 
Section 87407 and 87100 prohibits officials from making, participating in making, or using their 
position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating employment, or 
have any arrangement concerning employment. These provisions are commonly referred to as 
the "revolving door" prohibitions. 
You have already left state service and are requesting advice solely on the permanent 
ban. For this reason, we do not address the one-year ban or conflicts of interest in arranging 
employment. 
The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating, for compensation, in certain proceedings involving the State of California and 
other specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former 
state employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 
18741.1.) The permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of 
absence from any particular office or employment. (Regulation 18741. l (a)( l ).) 


File No. A-15-040 
Page No. 3 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or 
any oral or written communication - or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or 
communication - made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other 
proceeding in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. 
, .. Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a 
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, 
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state 
administrative agency ... . " (Section 87400(c).) 
Additionally, an official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she 
took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, 
disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, 
investigation, or use of confidential information . ... " (Section 87400(d).) 
The situations you describe do not qualify as "judicial, quasi-judicial, or other 
proceedings" for purposes of the permanent ban. Each appears to be investigative proceedings to 
determine the feasibility of the programs. There were no particular parties involved and no action 
was taken either at the time or for the following four years. The Act does not prevent your 
consulting with a private company regarding these projects. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
HMR:jgl 
Sincerely, 
John W. Wallace 
Assistant General Counsel 
WM\ 
By: 
Heather M. Rowan 
Counsel, Legal Division

private consultant who will be contracting with your former agency?

No. The projects on which you will be engaged are different proceedings from the 
projects you worked on during your state employment.

CONCLUSION: No. The projects on which you will be engaged are different proceedings from the 
projects you worked on during your state employment.
